mission.title: Install and Configure Parsec on Kubernetes
mission.description: PARSEC is an open-source initiative that provides a common API for hardware security and cryptographic services in a platform-agnostic way. Installing PARSEC allows you to manage keys and perform cryptographic operations without needing to know the underlying hardware details.

steps.title: Add the Parsec Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Parsec Helm repository to your Helm configuration. This allows you to install Parsec using Helm charts.
```bash
helm repo add parallaxsecond https://parallaxsecond.github.io/parsec-helm
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Parsec using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install the Parsec service using Helm. Make sure to create a namespace for it.
```bash
helm install parsec parallaxsecond/parsec --namespace parsec --create-namespace --version 1.4.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Parsec pods are running successfully in the parsec namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace parsec
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure that the Parsec service runs efficiently, set resource limits for the deployment. Edit the deployment configuration:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment parsec --namespace parsec
```
Add the following resource limits under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for local access
steps.description:
If you want to access the Parsec service locally, set up port forwarding:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/parsec --namespace parsec 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Parsec will show the Parsec pods in the 'Running' state when you execute `kubectl get pods --namespace parsec`. You should also be able to access the service locally via port 8080.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add parallaxsecond https://parallaxsecond.github.io/parsec-helm
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
